title: MTA ridership Analysis
text:
1 Introduction
The subway is one of the most iconic parts of New York City! It’s an iconic and historic mode of transportation that has made its way into every New Yorker’s life. So much so, that there are entire art movements dedicated to life in New York on the subway lines. A major chunk of critical commentary about the NYC subway is the delays and maintenance activities. The subway trains go into delays sometimes unexpectedly, usually due to something blocking the track, sometimes due to individuals activities on these subways etc.
These delays cause major problems to all those who use the subway on a regular basis, they need to find alternate modes of transport, and are delayed into getting on with their lives on quite a regular basis.
This study is aimed at finding the patterns in these delays, which may be cruicial for city policy makers, and residents alike, about the actual frequency and intensity of delays across different subway lines, through the course of a long period of time, along with the reasons for these delays.

title: 2  Data
text:
2.1 Description
There are four main datasets that we plan to use for this project. All of the data is collected by the Metropolitan Transportation Authority, and were imported by directly downloading the CSV files from the official website.

title: 2  Data
text:
2.1.1 MTA Subway Trains Delayed: Beginning 2020
This dataset (source) contains the number and percentage of subway trains delayed per weekday, broken down into specific delay categories (from 2020 onward). According to the source, the data on train arrivals is manually entered into a digital dispatching tool, and the number of trains delayed by more than five minutes or missing scheduled stops is summed up. Additionally, every delay is manually assigned a category and subcategory. Thus, the assignment of categories to delays depends on the subjective judgment of the dispatcher, and in some cases, the delays may be caused by more than one cause (which cannot be recorded in this format). The frequency of updates to this dataset varies widely, but it has been updated at least once every month. As of 11/19/2024, the dataset has 39051 rows and 7 columns.
The following columns are included in the data:

month - The month in which the delays occur
division - The subway line division, lettered or numbered
line - The subway line
day_type - Whether the day is a weekday or a weekend
reporting_category - One of six categories causing the delays
subcategory - The subcategory of the delays
delays - The number of train delays for the category and time period



2.1.2 MTA Service Alerts: Beginning April 2020
This dataset (source) contains details of service alerts causing route changes in the MTA from April 2020 onward. Note that this contains service changes of MTA buses as well, but for now we are interested in the MTA subway alone. Real-time service alerts are first drafted within the MTA alert system, and then disseminated via various channels in real time, while planned alerts are entered into the system ahead of time. The frequency of updates to this dataset varies, but it has been updated at least once every month on average. As of 11/19/2024, the dataset has 351631 rows and 9 columns.
The following columns are included in the data:

Alert ID
Event ID
Update Number
Date
Agency - Transit agency associated with the event (we are interested in the NYC subway)
Status Label - Pipe-delimited list of service statuses
Affected - Pipe-delimited list of affected routes
Header - High-level overview of service impact
Description - Additional information related to the alert



2.1.3 MTA Subway Wait Assessment: Beginning 2020
This dataset (source) measures how regularly trains are spaced on the MTA subway. Subway schedule data, including service changes, and actual train movement for each day are compiled into a pair of timetables, while the actual train movement and distance data come from a variety of sources. The dataset appears to have been updated at least once every month, and the data has 5306 rows and 8 columns as of 11/19/2024.
The following columns are included in the data:

month
division - The subway line division, lettered or numbered
line - The subway line
day_type - Whether the day is a weekday or a weekend
period - Whether peak or off-peak service
num_timepoints_passing_wait_assessment - Number of scheduled timepoints where trips pass the wait assessment standard
num_sched_timepoints - Number of scheduled timepoints
wait assessment - Percentage of trips that pass the wait assessment



2.1.4 MTA Daily Ridership Data: Beginning 2020
This dataset (source) provides ridership and traffic estimates for subways, buses, the LIRR and other public transport, beginning 3/1/2020. A percentage comparison against a pre-pandemic date is also given in the dataset. The subway data in this dataset (which we are primarily interested in) are determined from MetroCard and OMNY swipes/taps at stations. In contrast to the other datasets, this dataset appears to be updated once every weekday. As of 11/19/2024, the dataset has 1724 rows and 15 columns.
The following columns are included in the data:

Date
Subways: Total Estimated Ridership
Subways: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
Buses: Total Estimated Ridership
Buses: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
LIRR: Total Estimated Ridership
LIRR: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
Metro-North: Total Estimated Ridership
Metro-North: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
Access-A-Ride: Total Scheduled Trips
Access-A-Ride: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
Bridges and Tunnels: Total Traffic
Bridges and Tunnels: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
Staten Island Railway: Total Estimated Ridership
Staten Island Railway: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day

title: 2  Data
text:
2.2 Missing value analysis

2.2.1 MTA Subway Trains Delayed: Beginning 2020


Code
subway_train_delays <- read_csv("data/MTA_Subway_Trains_Delayed__Beginning_2020_20241118.csv")
plot_missing(subway_train_delays, num_char = 8)









Here, the subcategory field is missing in less than 12.5% of the rows, while all the other values are present in all rows.


2.2.2 MTA Service Alerts: Beginning April 2020


Code
mta_service_alerts <- read_csv("data/MTA_Service_Alerts__Beginning_April_2020_20241118.csv")
plot_missing(mta_service_alerts, num_char = 7, max_cols = 4)









Here, in about 62.5% of the rows only the Description field is missing, while there are a few rows where the Affected and the Header fields are missing as well.


2.2.3 MTA Subway Wait Assessment: Beginning 2020


Code
subway_wait_assessment <- read_csv("data/MTA_Subway_Wait_Assessment__Beginning_2020_20241119.csv")
plot_missing(subway_wait_assessment, num_char = 7)









This dataset has no missing values.


2.2.4 MTA Daily Ridership Data: Beginning 2020


Code
mta_daily_ridership <- read_csv("data/MTA_Daily_Ridership_Data__Beginning_2020_20241120.csv")
plot_missing(mta_daily_ridership, num_char = 5, max_cols = 8)









This dataset has no missing values.

subway_train_delays |> head()

Code
delays_category <- subway_train_delays[,!names(subway_train_delays) %in% c("subcategory")]




Code
pdf |> head()


                                                                        
1 function (file = if (onefile) "Rplots.pdf" else "Rplot%03d.pdf",      
2     width, height, onefile, family, title, fonts, version, paper,     
3     encoding, bg, fg, pointsize, pagecentre, colormodel, useDingbats, 
4     useKerning, fillOddEven, compress)                                
5 {                                                                     
6     initPSandPDFfonts()                                               




Code
pdf<- delays_category |> group_by(line, reporting_category, month) |> summarise(delays = sum(delays))


`summarise()` has grouped output by 'line', 'reporting_category'. You can
override using the `.groups` argument.




Code
ggplot(pdf, aes(x = month, y = delays)) +
  #geom_line(size = 1, color = "blue") +
  geom_point(size = 1, color = "cornflowerblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Delays Over Time by Line",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Delays"
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~line, scales = "free_y") +  # Facet over 'line', allowing independent y-axis scales
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 14),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none"
  )











Code
max_delays <- pdf %>%
  group_by(month) %>%
  summarize(max_delay = sum(delays))

# Create the plot
ggplot(max_delays, aes(x = month, y = max_delay)) +
  geom_line(size = 1, color = "red") +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "darkred") +
  labs(
    title = "Maximum Delays per Month",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Maximum Delay"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 14),
    legend.position = "none"
  )


Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0.
ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead.











Code
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Identify the line with the maximum delays per month
max_delays_lines <- pdf %>%
  group_by(month) %>%
  filter(delays == max(delays)) %>%
  ungroup()

# Plot the maximum delays per month with their respective lines
ggplot(max_delays_lines, aes(x = month, y = delays, color = line, group = line)) +
  #geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "Lines with Maximum Delays per Month",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Delays",
    color = "Line"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 14),
    legend.position = "right"
  )











Code
library(dplyr)

# Define a mapping of subway lines to their respective groups
subway_groups <- data.frame(
  line = c("1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7", "A", "C", "E", "B", "D", "F", "M", "G", "J", "Z", "L", "N", "Q", "R", "W"),
  group = c(
    "IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line",  # 1, 2, 3
    "IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line", 
    "IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line", 
    "IRT - Lexington Avenue Line",         # 4, 5, 6
    "IRT - Lexington Avenue Line", 
    "IRT - Lexington Avenue Line", 
    "IRT - Flushing Line",                 # 7
    "IND - Eighth Avenue Line",            # A, C, E
    "IND - Eighth Avenue Line", 
    "IND - Eighth Avenue Line", 
    "IND - Sixth Avenue Line",             # B, D, F, M
    "IND - Sixth Avenue Line", 
    "IND - Sixth Avenue Line", 
    "IND - Sixth Avenue Line", 
    "IND - Crosstown Line",                # G
    "BMT - Nassau Street Line",            # J, Z
    "BMT - Nassau Street Line", 
    "BMT - Canarsie Line",                 # L
    "BMT - Broadway Line",                 # N, Q, R, W
    "BMT - Broadway Line", 
    "BMT - Broadway Line", 
    "BMT - Broadway Line"
  )
)

# Join the original dataset with the subway groups
pdf_with_groups <- pdf %>%
  left_join(subway_groups, by = "line")

# View the updated dataset
head(pdf_with_groups)


# A tibble: 6 × 5
# Groups:   line, reporting_category [1]
  line  reporting_category month      delays group                             
  <chr> <chr>              <date>      <dbl> <chr>                             
1 1     Crew Availability  2020-01-01     27 IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line
2 1     Crew Availability  2020-02-01     22 IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line
3 1     Crew Availability  2020-03-01     89 IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line
4 1     Crew Availability  2020-04-01    221 IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line
5 1     Crew Availability  2020-05-01     41 IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line
6 1     Crew Availability  2020-06-01     96 IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line




Code
library(ggplot2)

# Create the plot faceted by subway groups
ggplot(pdf_with_groups, aes(x = month, y = delays, color = line, group = line)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~group, scales = "free_y") +  # Facet by subway groups with independent y-axes
  labs(
    title = "Delays Over Time Faceted by Subway Groups",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Delays",
    color = "Line"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 14),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )











Code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Calculate total delays per group and month
total_delays_per_group <- pdf_with_groups %>%
  group_by(group, month) %>%
  summarize(total_delays = sum(delays, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Create the faceted plot
ggplot(total_delays_per_group, aes(x = month, y = total_delays)) +
  geom_line(size = 0.5, color = "blue") +
  geom_point(size = 1, color = "darkblue") +
  facet_wrap(~group, scales = "free_y") +  # Facet by subway groups with independent y-axes
  labs(
    title = "Total Delays Per Month Faceted by Subway Groups",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Total Delays"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 14),
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "none"
  )











Code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Calculate total delays per group and month
total_delays_per_group <- pdf_with_groups %>%
  group_by(group, month) %>%
  summarize(total_delays = sum(delays, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Define custom colors for the subway groups (based on NYC Subway colors)
subway_group_colors <- c(
  "IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line" = "#EE352E", # Red
  "IRT - Lexington Avenue Line" = "#00933C",       # Green
  "IRT - Flushing Line" = "#B933AD",               # Purple
  "IND - Eighth Avenue Line" = "#0039A6",          # Blue
  "IND - Sixth Avenue Line" = "#FF6319",           # Orange
  "IND - Crosstown Line" = "#6CBE45",              # Light Green
  "BMT - Nassau Street Line" = "#996633",          # Brown
  "BMT - Canarsie Line" = "#A7A9AC",               # Gray
  "BMT - Broadway Line" = "#FCCC0A"                # Yellow
)

# Create the plot
ggplot(total_delays_per_group, aes(x = month, y = total_delays, color = group, group = group)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  #geom_point(size = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(values = subway_group_colors) +
  labs(
    title = "Total Delays Per Month by Subway Group",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Total Delays",
    color = "Subway Group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 14),
    legend.position = "right"
  )











Code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Calculate total delays per group and month
total_delays_per_group <- pdf_with_groups %>%
  group_by(group, month) %>%
  summarize(total_delays = sum(delays, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

# Define custom colors for the subway groups (based on NYC Subway colors)
subway_group_colors <- c(
  "IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line" = "#EE352E", # Red
  "IRT - Lexington Avenue Line" = "#00933C",       # Green
  "IRT - Flushing Line" = "#B933AD",               # Purple
  "IND - Eighth Avenue Line" = "#0039A6",          # Blue
  "IND - Sixth Avenue Line" = "#FF6319",           # Orange
  "IND - Crosstown Line" = "#6CBE45",              # Light Green
  "BMT - Nassau Street Line" = "#996633",          # Brown
  "BMT - Canarsie Line" = "#A7A9AC",               # Gray 
  "BMT - Broadway Line" = "#FCCC0A"                # Yellow
)

# Define custom legend labels, showing group name and corresponding lines
custom_legend_labels <- c(
  "IRT - Broadway–Seventh Avenue Line" = "1, 2, 3",
  "IRT - Lexington Avenue Line" = "4, 5, 6",
  "IRT - Flushing Line" = "Flushing (7)",
  "IND - Eighth Avenue Line" = "A, C, E",
  "IND - Sixth Avenue Line" = "B, D, F, M",
  "IND - Crosstown Line" = "Crosstown (G)",
  "BMT - Nassau Street Line" = "J, Z",
  "BMT - Canarsie Line" = "L",
  "BMT - Broadway Line" = "N, Q, R, W"
)

# Create the plot
ggplot(total_delays_per_group, aes(x = month, y = total_delays, color = group, group = group)) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  
  scale_color_manual(values = subway_group_colors, labels = custom_legend_labels) +
  labs(
    title = "Total Delays Per Month by Subway Group",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Total Delays",
    color = "Subway Group (Lines)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    text = element_text(size = 14),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )











Code
nyc_subway_lines <- c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E", "F", "G", "J", "Z", "L", "M", 
                      "N", "Q", "R", "W", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7", "S")

# Filter dataframe
filtered_df <- mta_service_alerts %>%
  group_by(`Alert ID`) %>%  # Group by Alert ID (or other identifier)
  filter(all(nyc_subway_lines %in% Affected)) %>%
  ungroup()




Code
nyc_subway_lines <- c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E", "F", "G", "J", "Z", "L", "M", 
                      "N", "Q", "R", "W", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5", "6", "7", "S")


linewise_service_alerts <- mta_service_alerts |> separate_rows(Affected, sep = "\\|") |> 
  filter(Affected %in% nyc_subway_lines)




Code
linewise_service_alerts |> head()

       <dbl>      <dbl>           <dbl> <chr>     <chr>  <chr>          <chr>

Code
# Create the plot




Code
ggplot(linewise_service_alerts, aes(x = Affected)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "steelblue", color = "black") +  # Bar chart
  facet_wrap(~ `Status Label`, scales = "free_y") +  # Facet by Status Label
  theme_minimal() +  # Minimal theme
  labs(
    title = "Frequency of Affected Subway Lines by Status Label",
    x = "Subway Line",
    y = "Frequency"
  ) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))  # Rotate x-axis labels

title: 2  Data
text:
2.1 Description
There are four main datasets that we plan to use for this project. All of the data is collected by the Metropolitan Transportation Authority, and were imported by directly downloading the CSV files from the official website.

This dataset (source) provides ridership and traffic estimates for subways, buses, the LIRR and other public transport, beginning 3/1/2020. A percentage comparison against a pre-pandemic date is also given in the dataset. The subway data in this dataset (which we are primarily interested in) are determined from MetroCard and OMNY swipes/taps at stations. In contrast to the other datasets, this dataset appears to be updated once every weekday. As of 11/19/2024, the dataset has 1724 rows and 15 columns.
The following columns are included in the data:

Date
Subways: Total Estimated Ridership
Subways: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
Buses: Total Estimated Ridership
Buses: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
LIRR: Total Estimated Ridership
LIRR: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
Metro-North: Total Estimated Ridership
Metro-North: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
Access-A-Ride: Total Scheduled Trips
Access-A-Ride: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
Bridges and Tunnels: Total Traffic
Bridges and Tunnels: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day
Staten Island Railway: Total Estimated Ridership
Staten Island Railway: % of Comparable Pre-Pandemic Day

title: 2  Data
text:
2.2 Missing value analysis

2.2.1 MTA Subway Trains Delayed: Beginning 2020


Code
subway_train_delays <- read_csv("data/MTA_Subway_Trains_Delayed__Beginning_2020_20241118.csv")
plot_missing(subway_train_delays, num_char = 8)









Here, the subcategory field is missing in less than 12.5% of the rows, while all the other values are present in all rows.


2.2.2 MTA Service Alerts: Beginning April 2020


Code
mta_service_alerts <- read_csv("data/MTA_Service_Alerts__Beginning_April_2020_20241118.csv")
plot_missing(mta_service_alerts, num_char = 7, max_cols = 4)









Here, in about 62.5% of the rows only the Description field is missing, while there are a few rows where the Affected and the Header fields are missing as well.


2.2.3 MTA Subway Wait Assessment: Beginning 2020


Code
subway_wait_assessment <- read_csv("data/MTA_Subway_Wait_Assessment__Beginning_2020_20241119.csv")
plot_missing(subway_wait_assessment, num_char = 7)









This dataset has no missing values.


2.2.4 MTA Daily Ridership Data: Beginning 2020


Code
mta_daily_ridership <- read_csv("data/MTA_Daily_Ridership_Data__Beginning_2020_20241120.csv")
plot_missing(mta_daily_ridership, num_char = 5, max_cols = 8)









This dataset has no missing values.
